title: What exactly is an LLM?
description: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a statistical engine trained to predict the next token. At FAANG scale, this means understanding architecture, training pipelines, inference optimization, alignment, and system design.

sections.title: The Core Objective: Next-Token Prediction
sections.description: An LLM takes a sequence of tokens and outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary for the next token. Generation is auto-regressive — each predicted token is appended to the input for the next step. This simple objective, applied at massive scale, yields emergent reasoning, coding, and multilingual capabilities.

sections.title: Tokenization — BPE, SentencePiece & Vocabulary Design
sections.description:
Tokenization is the first and surprisingly impactful step in LLM pipelines. It defines the vocabulary — the set of subword units the model sees. FAANG interviews test whether you understand tokenization artifacts and vocabulary engineering.

Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE): Start with individual bytes/characters, iteratively merge the most frequent adjacent pair. Merged pair becomes a new token in the vocabulary. Continue until desired vocabulary size (typically 32K-128K). Used by GPT models, Llama 2/3 (although Llama 3 uses a tiktoken-based BPE).

SentencePiece (Unigram): Start with a large pool of candidate subwords, iteratively prune the least useful one (measured by likelihood loss). Better for multilingual models because it doesn't require pre-tokenized words. Used by Gemma, PaLM, T5.

Critical tokenization artifacts to know:
• Number tokenization: '123' may tokenize as ['1', '23'] or ['12', '3'] depending on frequency. This makes LLMs bad at arithmetic — each split sees different training examples.
• Whitespace sensitivity: Llama 2 tokenizer treats 'hello' and ' hello' differently (the space is a separate token). This can cause subtle bugs when formatting prompts.
• Multilingual inefficiency: English gets ~1 token/word, languages like Thai or Vietnamese get 2-3× more tokens per word → effectively reduces context window and increases cost for non-English users.
• Special tokens: <|begin_of_text|>, <|end_of_text|>, padding — must be reserved at vocabulary IDs 0, 1, etc.

Llama 3 tokenizer details: Uses tiktoken (BPE, OpenAI-style). Vocab size: 128K. Trained on ~500K byte sequences. Supports all UTF-8 inputs. Much better at multilingual and code than Llama 2's SentencePiece tokenizer.
sections.codeBlock:
# Counting tokens with tiktoken (OpenAI/Llama 3 tokenizer)
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding('cl100k_base')  # GPT-4, Llama 3 compatible

text = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
tokens = enc.encode(text)
print(f"Text: {text}")
print(f"Tokens: {tokens}")
print(f"Decoded: {enc.decode(tokens)}")
print(f"Token count: {len(tokens)}")

# Demonstrating tokenization artifacts
numbers = '123456789'
print(f"\n'{numbers}' → {enc.encode(numbers)}")  # may split unexpectedly

# Special tokens
special = '<|begin_of_text|>Hello<|end_of_text|>'
print(f"Special tokens in '{special}' → {enc.encode(special)}")

# Vocabulary size
print(f"\nVocabulary size: {enc.n_vocab}")

# ponytail: real tokenizer evaluation checks parity across languages;
# measure tokens-per-word ratio for English vs Thai vs Arabic

sections.title: Scaling Laws (Chinchilla, 2022) — FAANG Favourite
sections.description:
Kaplan et al. (2020) showed that loss scales as a power law with model size N, dataset size D, and compute C. DeepMind's Chinchilla paper (Hoffmann et al., 2022) refined this: for a given compute budget, model size and training tokens should scale equally (~20 tokens per parameter). This invalidated GPT-3's design (too large, too few tokens) and led to Llama/Gemma being compute-optimal at smaller sizes with more data.

Rule of thumb: D_optimal ≈ 20 × N. A 7B model → train on ~140B tokens. A 70B model → train on ~1.4T tokens.

Why FAANG cares: Drives decisions on whether to train a new model vs. fine-tune, and at what scale.
sections.codeBlock:
# Chinchilla loss formula (simplified)
# L(N, D) = E + A/N^α + B/D^β
# E = irreducible entropy, α ≈ β ≈ 0.5

def compute_optimal_tokens(num_params):
    """Chinchilla-optimal training token count."""
    # ponytail: 20x rule of thumb, real exponents vary by domain
    return 20 * num_params

print(compute_optimal_tokens(7e9))   # 140B tokens for 7B model
print(compute_optimal_tokens(70e9))  # 1.4T tokens for 70B model

sections.title: The Transformer Architecture
sections.description: The decoder-only Transformer (GPT-style) is the dominant LLM architecture. Unlike RNNs which process tokens sequentially, Transformers process the entire sequence in parallel via Self-Attention. Each of the N stacked blocks contains: Multi-Head Self-Attention (captures relationships) → Add & LayerNorm → Feed-Forward Network (FFN) → Add & LayerNorm.

sections.title: Attention Variants: MHA → MQA → GQA
sections.description:
The biggest architectural knob for inference efficiency is how many KV heads you use. FAANG interviews frequently ask you to compare and calculate memory costs.

MHA (Multi-Head Attention): Each of H heads has its own Q, K, V. Memory = 2 × H × d_model per token per layer.
MQA (Multi-Query Attention): All Q heads share a single K and single V head. Memory = 2 × 1 × d_model — H× reduction. Faster but can hurt quality (used in Falcon).
GQA (Grouped-Query Attention): G groups, each group shares K/V. Memory = 2 × G × d_model where G ≪ H. Best quality/speed tradeoff. Used in Llama 2 70B, Llama 3, Mistral, Gemma.

Example: Llama 3 8B: H=32 Q heads, G=8 KV heads → 4× KV cache reduction vs MHA.
sections.codeBlock:
# GQA: group Q heads, share K/V within each group
def grouped_query_attention(x, num_q_heads, num_kv_heads):
    B, T, C = x.shape
    q_head_dim = C // num_q_heads
    groups = num_q_heads // num_kv_heads  # e.g. 32//8 = 4

    Q = linear(x, W_Q).view(B, T, num_q_heads, q_head_dim)
    K = linear(x, W_K).view(B, T, num_kv_heads, q_head_dim)
    V = linear(x, W_V).view(B, T, num_kv_heads, q_head_dim)

    # Expand K, V to match Q heads by repeating each group
    K = K.repeat_interleave(groups, dim=2)  # [B,T,H,d]
    V = V.repeat_interleave(groups, dim=2)

    scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2,-1)) / math.sqrt(q_head_dim)
    return F.softmax(scores, dim=-1) @ V

sections.title: The Training Pipeline: Pre-train → SFT → Alignment
sections.description:
Modern LLMs are built in three stages:

1. Pre-training: Next-token prediction on trillions of tokens (Common Crawl, GitHub, books). Creates a Base Model with world knowledge. Compute-intensive — GPT-4 estimated at ~$100M.

2. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): Train on high-quality (instruction, response) pairs. Shifts the model from document-completion to instruction-following. Requires ~10K–100K examples.

3. Alignment — RLHF or DPO:
• RLHF: Train a Reward Model on human preference pairs (preferred vs rejected). Use PPO to optimize the LLM policy against the reward model + KL divergence penalty from SFT baseline.
• DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): Skips the reward model — directly optimizes on preference pairs using a closed-form loss. Simpler, more stable, less compute. Used in Llama 3 Instruct.
sections.codeBlock:
# DPO loss (simplified)
# Maximizes log-ratio of policy likelihood: preferred vs rejected
def dpo_loss(policy_model, ref_model, preferred, rejected, beta=0.1):
    # Log probs from current policy
    log_p_w = policy_model.log_prob(preferred)
    log_p_l = policy_model.log_prob(rejected)

    # Log probs from frozen SFT reference
    log_r_w = ref_model.log_prob(preferred)
    log_r_l = ref_model.log_prob(rejected)

    # Implicit reward: β * log(π/π_ref)
    reward_w = beta * (log_p_w - log_r_w)
    reward_l = beta * (log_p_l - log_r_l)

    # Binary cross-entropy over preference pairs
    loss = -F.logsigmoid(reward_w - reward_l).mean()
    return loss

sections.title: RLHF Deep Dive — Reward Modeling & PPO
sections.description:
RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) is how LLMs are aligned to human preferences. While DPO is simpler, RLHF with PPO is still used by OpenAI (GPT-4, o1), Anthropic (Claude), and Google (Gemini). FAANG interviews test your understanding of the full RLHF pipeline.

Step 1: SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning). Start with a pre-trained base model. Fine-tune on high-quality (instruction, response) pairs. This creates a model that follows instructions but may not produce preferred outputs.

Step 2: Reward Model (RM) Training. Take the SFT model, remove the LM head, replace with a regression head outputting a scalar reward. Train on preference pairs (response_A, response_B, label: preferred) using the Bradley-Terry model: P(A beats B) = σ(r_A - r_B). Loss = -log σ(r_w - r_l). The RM learns to predict which response a human would prefer.

Step 3: PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization). Optimize the SFT model (now called the 'policy') against the frozen RM. Key formula:

L(θ) = -E[r_θ(x,y) - β × KL(π_θ || π_ref)]

Where r_θ is the reward for response y given prompt x (scaled by the RM), and the KL penalty prevents the policy from diverging too far from the SFT baseline (which would lead to reward hacking — the model generating nonsensical text that scores high reward).

PPO-Clip (the 'P' in PPO): The policy update is clipped to prevent destructive updates: ratio = π_θ(y|x) / π_old(y|x), clipped to [1-ε, 1+ε]. This prevents a single bad update from destroying the model. ε = 0.2 is standard.

Important nuance: The KL penalty in PPO is usually adaptive — if KL is too low (model isn't learning), decrease β. If KL is too high (model is diverging), increase β. This is called KL-adaptive PPO or 'PPO-kl'.
sections.codeBlock:
# Reward model training (Bradley-Terry preference model)
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def reward_model_loss(rm, preffered_responses, rejected_responses):
    """
    rm: a model that outputs a scalar reward for each response.
    Returns: negative log-likelihood that preferred > rejected.
    """
    r_w = rm(preffered_responses)   # [B, 1] — reward for preferred
    r_l = rm(rejected_responses)    # [B, 1] — reward for rejected
    # Bradley-Terry: P(wins) = sigmoid(r_w - r_l)
    loss = -F.logsigmoid(r_w - r_l).mean()
    # Accuracy: what fraction of pairs have r_w > r_l?
    accuracy = (r_w > r_l).float().mean().item()
    return loss, accuracy

# PPO-Clip update (simplified core)
def ppo_update(policy, ref_policy, reward_model, prompts, responses, epsilon=0.2):
    """
    policy: current model (π_θ)
    ref_policy: frozen SFT model (π_ref)
    """
    # Current policy log-prob
    log_probs = policy.log_prob(prompts, responses)         # π_θ
    # Reference policy log-prob (frozen)
    with torch.no_grad():
        ref_log_probs = ref_policy.log_prob(prompts, responses)  # π_ref
    
    # Reward from RM
    rewards = reward_model(prompts, responses)              # r_θ
    
    # KL penalty (per-token)
    kl_div = log_probs - ref_log_probs                      # β × KL
    
    # Policy ratio for clipping
    ratio = (log_probs - ref_log_probs).exp()               # π_θ / π_ref
    clipped_ratio = torch.clamp(ratio, 1 - epsilon, 1 + epsilon)
    
    # PPO-Clip objective
    advantages = rewards - kl_div.mean()                     # normalize
    policy_loss = -torch.min(ratio * advantages,
                              clipped_ratio * advantages).mean()
    # Include KL in total loss
    total_loss = policy_loss + (kl_div * 0.01).mean()
    return total_loss

# ponytail: real PPO uses advantage estimation (GAE) and value function critic

sections.title: Sampling Strategies — Temperature, Top-k, Top-p
sections.description:
Once the LM head outputs logits over the vocabulary, you need a decoding strategy. This is a common system-design question at FAANG.

Greedy: argmax. Deterministic but repetitive — no diversity.
Temperature (τ): Divide logits by τ before softmax. τ<1 → sharper/more confident. τ>1 → flatter/more creative. τ=0 → greedy.
Top-k: Keep only the k highest-probability tokens, renormalize. Simple but k is hard to tune across distributions.
Top-p (Nucleus): Keep smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability ≥ p (e.g. 0.9). Adapts to the distribution shape — better than top-k in practice.
Beam Search: Maintains B candidate sequences, expands each, keeps top-B. Deterministic, good for translation/summarization. Bad for open-ended generation (repetitive).
sections.codeBlock:
def sample_next_token(logits, temperature=1.0, top_k=0, top_p=0.9):
    # 1. Temperature scaling
    logits = logits / max(temperature, 1e-8)

    # 2. Top-k filtering
    if top_k > 0:
        kth = torch.topk(logits, top_k).values[-1]
        logits[logits < kth] = float('-inf')

    # 3. Top-p (nucleus) filtering
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
    sorted_probs, sorted_idx = torch.sort(probs, descending=True)
    cum_probs = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    # Remove tokens once cumulative prob exceeds top_p
    remove = cum_probs - sorted_probs > top_p
    sorted_probs[remove] = 0
    probs = torch.zeros_like(probs).scatter_(0, sorted_idx, sorted_probs)
    probs /= probs.sum()

    return torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)

sections.title: Quantization — INT8, INT4, GPTQ, AWQ
sections.description:
Quantization reduces model weight precision to save VRAM and speed up inference. Critical for deploying 70B+ models on consumer hardware.

Post-Training Quantization (PTQ): No retraining needed. Round weights to INT8 (2× compression) or INT4 (4× compression). Accuracy degrades at INT4 without calibration.
GPTQ: Layer-wise quantization using Hessian-based rounding. Minimizes per-layer quantization error. Gold standard for INT4 LLMs.
AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantization): Protects the 1% of weights that correspond to high-activation channels — maintains accuracy better than GPTQ at same bit-width. Used in Gemma, Mistral deployments.

Memory math: A 70B FP16 model = 70B × 2 bytes = 140 GB VRAM. With INT4 → 35 GB — fits on 2× A100 40GB.
sections.codeBlock:
# Naive symmetric INT8 quantization
def quantize_int8(weight: torch.Tensor):
    # Scale factor: maps max abs value to 127
    scale = weight.abs().max() / 127.0
    q = torch.round(weight / scale).clamp(-128, 127).to(torch.int8)
    return q, scale

def dequantize_int8(q, scale):
    return q.to(torch.float32) * scale

# Memory comparison
params = 70e9
fp16_gb  = params * 2 / 1e9   # 140 GB
int8_gb  = params * 1 / 1e9   # 70 GB
int4_gb  = params * 0.5 / 1e9 # 35 GB
print(f'FP16: {fp16_gb:.0f}GB | INT8: {int8_gb:.0f}GB | INT4: {int4_gb:.0f}GB')

sections.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning: When to Use What
sections.description:
A core ML system design question at FAANG: given a use case, should you use RAG, fine-tuning, or both?

CriterionRAGFine-Tuning
Knowledge sourceExternal, updatableBaked into weights
Hallucination riskLower (grounded)Higher
LatencyHigher (retrieval step)Lower
Output style/formatHard to controlEasy to control
Data freshnessReal-timeStale at cutoff
CostInfra + embeddingOne-time GPU cost

Rule: Use RAG for factual Q&A over private/live data. Use fine-tuning for style, format, domain-specific reasoning. Use both for enterprise assistants.
sections.codeBlock:
# Minimal RAG pipeline
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import faiss, numpy as np

class SimpleRAG:
    def __init__(self, docs, embed_model='all-MiniLM-L6-v2'):
        self.docs = docs
        self.embedder = SentenceTransformer(embed_model)
        vecs = self.embedder.encode(docs).astype('float32')
        self.index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(vecs.shape[1])
        self.index.add(vecs / np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True))

    def retrieve(self, query, top_k=3):
        q = self.embedder.encode([query]).astype('float32')
        q /= np.linalg.norm(q)
        _, idx = self.index.search(q, top_k)
        return [self.docs[i] for i in idx[0]]

    def answer(self, query, llm):
        context = '\n'.join(self.retrieve(query))
        prompt = f'Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {query}\nAnswer:'
        return llm.generate(prompt)

sections.title: Hallucination: Causes & Mitigations
sections.description:
Hallucination — the model generating factually incorrect but confident text — is a core reliability concern at FAANG scale.

Root causes:
• Knowledge gaps: facts not in pre-training data
• Sycophancy: RLHF reward hacking toward confident-sounding answers
• Exposure bias: training on ground truth, generating from own outputs
• Context overload: long contexts cause attention to drift from key facts

Mitigations:
• RAG: Ground responses in retrieved documents
• Self-consistency: Sample N outputs, majority vote (CoT)
• Citation forcing: Prompt/fine-tune to cite sources
• Logit calibration: Detect low-confidence tokens, abstain or hedge
• Constitutional AI: Model critiques its own outputs (Anthropic)
• FactScore: Automated fact-checking against knowledge base
sections.codeBlock:
# Self-consistency decoding (Wang et al., 2022)
def self_consistent_answer(question, llm, n_samples=10, temperature=0.7):
    # Sample N reasoning chains
    answers = []
    for _ in range(n_samples):
        chain = llm.generate(
            f'{question}\nLet\'s think step by step.',
            temperature=temperature
        )
        # Extract final answer from chain
        answer = extract_answer(chain)
        answers.append(answer)

    # Majority vote
    from collections import Counter
    return Counter(answers).most_common(1)[0][0]

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017) — The foundational Transformer paper that enables all modern LLMs
  • Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models (Kaplan et al., 2020) — Power-law scaling of loss with model size, data, and compute
  • Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla, Hoffmann et al., 2022) — 20-tokens-per-parameter scaling rule, FAANG interview favourite
  • Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models — Open-source LLM with GQA and RoPE, production architecture reference
  • Llama 3 Herd of Models — Latest Llama architecture: 128K context, 128K vocab, GQA at all scales
  • tiktoken — OpenAI's fast BPE tokenizer, used by Llama 3 and GPT-4

details:
Key Model Hyperparameters to Know: d_model (embedding dim), n_layers (depth), n_heads (attention heads), n_kv_heads (GQA groups), intermediate_size (FFN width), vocab_size, context_length. Llama 3 8B: d_model=4096, n_layers=32, n_heads=32, n_kv_heads=8, context=128K.

Emergent Abilities: Abilities that appear suddenly at scale (e.g., >100B params) rather than gradually — chain-of-thought reasoning, arithmetic, code synthesis. Debated in literature (Schaeffer et al., 2023 argue it's a measurement artifact).

Positional Encoding Variants: Sinusoidal (original), Learned (GPT-2), RoPE (Rotary, used in Llama/Mistral — rotates Q&K vectors in 2D subspaces, enabling relative position encoding and length extrapolation), ALiBi (adds linear bias to attention scores — extrapolates to longer sequences without retraining).
